html:
[634]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 飘泊长安 - Literally means 'wandering in Chang'an,' referring to a state of homelessness or being far from one's home.
  • 穷愁颇甚 - Means 'extremely poor and distressed,' describing a severe state of poverty and hardship.
  • 流落江湖十四春 - Literally means 'wandering in rivers and lakes for fourteen springs,' a metaphor for a long period of wandering and hardship.
  • 徐娘半老尚风尘 - Means 'an older woman still living in the world of courtesans,' referring to a woman who has aged but remains in a profession associated with pleasure and luxury.
  • 西楼一枕鸳鸯梦 - Means 'a dream of love in a western pavilion,' describing a romantic dream.
  • 明月窥窗也笑人 - Means 'the bright moon peering through the window laughs at me,' a poetic way of expressing loneliness or self-deprecation.
  • 含情不忍诉琵琶 - Means 'holding feelings unable to express them with a pipa (a Chinese lute),' describing a person who is too emotional to play the instrument.
  • 多谢西川贵公子 - Means 'thank the noble young man from Western Sichuan,' expressing gratitude to a wealthy and influential person.
  • 肯持红烛赏残花 - Means 'willing to hold a red candle to admire the remaining flowers,' a metaphor for appreciating beauty even in its decline.
  • 五十六字足抵一篇琵琶行矣 - Means 'these fifty-six characters are equivalent to a whole *Pipa Xing*,' comparing the brevity and depth of the poems to a famous work.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 胶州 - Refers to Jiaozhou, a city in Shandong Province.
  • 法南野 - The name of the person speaking, possibly a scholar or traveler.
  • 长安 - Refers to Chang'an, the ancient capital of China, known for its cultural and political significance.
  • 泺口 - A place in Shandong Province, known for its historical and cultural importance.
  • 琵琶行 - A famous poem by Bai Juyi, a renowned Tang Dynasty poet, known for its vivid portrayal of a courtesan's life.
  • 西川 - Refers to the western part of Sichuan Province, historically known for its wealth and influence.

Proper Nouns

  • 法南野 - The name of the speaker.
  • 李符千 - The name of the person whose seat the speaker is at, likely a respected figure.
  • 御史 - A title referring to a high-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy.
  • 泺口 - A place name in Shandong Province.
  • 琵琶行 - A famous poem by Bai Juyi.
  • 西川 - A region in Sichuan Province.

[635]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 嗜古如南涧 - Loves antiquity as much as Nanjian.
  • 博辩则过之 - More knowledgeable and eloquent than Nanjian.
  • 不幸夭逝 - Unfortunately died young.
  • 南涧乞余志其墓 - Nanjian asked me to write an epitaph for his tomb.
  • 匆匆未果 - Hurriedly and without success.
  • 并其事状失之 - And the details of his life were lost.
  • 至今以为憾也 - And it remains a regret to this day.
  • 一日，在余生云精舍讨论古礼 - One day, at my residence 'Yun Jing She,' we discussed ancient rites.
  • 因举所闻一事曰 - He then mentioned a story he had heard.
  • 博山有书生 - There was a scholar in Boshan.
  • 夜行林莽间 - Walking through the forest at night.
  • 见贵官坐松下 - He saw a high-ranking official sitting under a pine tree.
  • 呼与语 - He called out to him to speak.
  • 谛视乃其已故表丈某公也 - Upon closer inspection, it was his deceased maternal uncle, Mr. Mou.
  • 不得已近前拜谒 - He had no choice but to approach and pay respects.
  • 问家事甚悉 - He inquired about family matters in great detail.
  • 古称体魄藏于野 - It is said that the body is buried in the wild.
  • 而神依于庙主 - While the spirit relies on the ancestral tablet in the temple.
  • 丈人有家祠 - My uncle has a family shrine.
  • 何为在此 - Why is he here?
  • 此泥于古不墓祭之文也 - This is due to the ancient text that does not advocate for tomb sacrifices.
  • 夫庙祭地也 - The temple is a place for sacrifice.
  • 主祭位也 - The ancestral tablet is the place of worship.
  • 神之来格 - The spirit comes to be present.
  • 以是地是位为依归焉耳 - It is based on this place and this position.
  • 如神常居于庙 - If the spirit always resides in the temple.
  • 常附于主 - Always attached to the ancestral tablet.
  • 是世世祖妣与子孙人鬼杂处也 - It would mean that ancestors and descendants, humans and ghosts, live together.
  • 且有庙有主 - And if there is a temple and an ancestral tablet.
  • 为有爵禄者言之耳 - It is only for those with titles and salaries to say so.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 益都 - A place name, possibly referring to a region or city in ancient China.
  • 南涧 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical figure.
  • 博山 - A place name, possibly referring to a region or city in ancient China.
  • 庙祭 - Refers to the practice of ancestral worship in temples.
  • 家祠 - Refers to a family shrine or ancestral hall.
  • 不墓祭之文 - Refers to the ancient text that does not advocate for tomb sacrifices.
  • 爵禄 - Refers to titles and salaries, indicating a high social status.

Proper Nouns

  • 益都 - A place name.
  • 李生文渊 - A proper name.
  • 南涧 - A proper name.
  • 博山 - A place name.
  • 表丈某公 - A proper name, referring to the deceased official.

[636]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 能建廟者萬家不一二 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'among ten thousand families, barely a few can build a temple,' emphasizing the rarity of such acts.
  • 能立祠者千家不一二 - Similar to the above, meaning 'among a thousand families, barely a few can establish a shrine.'
  • 能設主者百家不一二 - Again, a hyperbolic expression meaning 'among a hundred families, barely a few can set up an ancestral tablet.'
  • 如神依主而不依墓 - Conditional statement meaning 'if the spirits depend on the ancestral tablet and not the tomb,' suggesting a belief in the importance of the tablet over the tomb.
  • 百千亿万贫贱之家 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'hundreds of thousands of millions of poor and humble families,' emphasizing the vast number of such families.
  • 其祖妣皆无依之鬼也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'their ancestors are all spirits without a place to depend on,' suggesting a state of neglect or abandonment.
  • 有是理耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is there such a thing?' used to express doubt or skepticism.
  • 知鬼神之情状者，莫若圣人 - Statement meaning 'no one knows the nature of the spirits better than the sage,' emphasizing the sage's superior knowledge.
  • 明器之礼，自夏后氏以来矣 - Statement meaning 'the ritual of spirit vessels has been practiced since the time of the Xia Dynasty,' indicating its long history.
  • 使神在主而不在墓，则明器当设于庙 - Conditional statement meaning 'if the spirits are in the ancestral tablet and not the tomb, then the spirit vessels should be placed in the temple,' suggesting a logical conclusion based on the belief.
  • 乃皆瘗之于墓中 - Statement meaning 'yet they are all buried in the tomb,' highlighting a contradiction between belief and practice.
  • 是以器供神，而置于神所不至也 - Conclusion meaning 'thus the vessels are offered to the spirits but placed where the spirits do not come,' suggesting a misplaced ritual.
  • 圣人顾若是颠耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'would the sage act so foolishly?' expressing surprise or disbelief.
  • 卫人之癙离之，殷礼也 - Statement meaning 'the people of Wei bury them separately, which is the custom of the Yin Dynasty,' indicating a historical practice.
  • 鲁人之癙合之，周礼也 - Statement meaning 'the people of Lu bury them together, which is the custom of the Zhou Dynasty,' indicating another historical practice.
  • 孔子善周 - Statement meaning 'Confucius favored the Zhou customs,' suggesting a preference for certain practices.
  • 使神不在墓，则墓之分合，了无所异 - Conditional statement meaning 'if the spirits are not in the tomb, then the separation or combination of tombs would make no difference,' suggesting a logical conclusion.
  • 有何善不善耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'what is good or bad about it?' expressing a neutral stance.
  • 礼曰：父殁而不忍读，父之书手泽存焉尔 - Quotation from ritual texts meaning 'rites say: when a father dies, one does not dare to read his books because the sweat marks of his hands remain,' emphasizing the emotional and physical connection to the deceased.
  • 母亡而不忍用其杯，睝口泽存焉尔 - Statement meaning 'when a mother dies, one does not dare to use her cup because the saliva marks of her mouth remain,' emphasizing the emotional and physical connection to the deceased.
  • 一物之微，尚且如是 - Statement meaning 'even the smallest object, it is still like this,' emphasizing the significance of small details.
  • 顾以先人体魄视如无物，而别植数寸之木，曰此吾父吾母之神也 - Statement meaning 'yet one treats the physical body of one's ancestors as nothing and instead plants a small piece of wood, saying this is the spirit of my father or mother,' expressing criticism of such practices.
  • 毋乃不知类耶 - Rhetorical question meaning 'is this not a failure to understand the proper categories?' expressing criticism.
  • 寺钟将动，且与子别，子今见吾，此后可毋为竖儒所惑矣 - Statement meaning 'the temple bell is about to ring, and I will soon part from you. You now see me, so in the future you will not be misled by petty scholars,' expressing a final admonition.
  • 生匆遽起立，东方已白，视之正其墓道前也 - Statement meaning 'the sun has risen, and you see that it is right in front of the tomb path,' indicating a change in location or perspective.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 建廟 - Refers to the construction of a temple, a significant act of devotion and respect.
  • 立祠 - Refers to the establishment of a shrine, another form of honoring ancestors.
  • 設主 - Refers to the setting up of an ancestral tablet, a symbol of the deceased's presence and memory.
  • 神依主而不依墓 - Reflects a belief in the importance of the ancestral tablet over the tomb, suggesting a spiritual connection.
  • 百千亿万贫贱之家 - Emphasizes the vast number of poor and humble families, highlighting the widespread neglect of ancestral worship.
  • 其祖妣皆无依之鬼也 - Suggests a state of neglect or abandonment, where the spirits of the ancestors are left without proper care.
  • 知鬼神之情状者，莫若圣人 - Refers to the sage's superior knowledge of the nature of spirits, a common theme in Confucian thought.
  • 明器之礼，自夏后氏以来矣 - Indicates the long history of the ritual of spirit vessels, dating back to the Xia Dynasty.
  • 使神在主而不在墓，则明器当设于庙 - Suggests a logical conclusion based on the belief that the spirits are in the ancestral tablet, not the tomb.
  • 乃皆瘗之于墓中 - Highlights a contradiction between belief and practice, where the vessels are buried in the tomb despite the belief that the spirits are in the tablet.
  • 是以器供神，而置于神所不至也 - Suggests a misplaced ritual, where the vessels are offered to the spirits but placed where the spirits do not come.
  • 圣人顾若是颠耶 - Expresses surprise or disbelief at the idea that the sage would act so foolishly.
  • 卫人之癙离之，殷礼也 - Refers to the historical practice of the people of Wei burying the deceased separately, following the customs of the Yin Dynasty.
  • 鲁人之癙合之，周礼也 - Refers to the historical practice of the people of Lu burying the deceased together, following the customs of the Zhou Dynasty.
  • 孔子善周 - Indicates Confucius's preference for the Zhou customs, suggesting a historical and cultural context.
  • 使神不在墓，则墓之分合，了无所异 - Suggests a logical conclusion that if the spirits are not in the tomb, the separation or combination of tombs would make no difference.
  • 有何善不善耶 - Expresses a neutral stance, questioning the significance of the practice.
  • 礼曰：父殁而不忍读，父之书手泽存焉尔 - Quotation from ritual texts emphasizing the emotional and physical connection to the deceased.
  • 母亡而不忍用其杯，睝口泽存焉尔 - Emphasizes the emotional and physical connection to the deceased, similar to the previous quotation.
  • 一物之微，尚且如是 - Emphasizes the significance of small details, suggesting a deep respect for the deceased.
  • 顾以先人体魄视如无物，而别植数寸之木，曰此吾父吾母之神也 - Criticizes the practice of treating the physical body of one's ancestors as nothing and instead planting a small piece of wood, suggesting a lack of proper respect.
  • 毋乃不知类耶 - Expresses criticism of the failure to understand the proper categories, suggesting a lack of knowledge or respect.
  • 寺钟将动，且与子别，子今见吾，此后可毋为竖儒所惑矣 - A final admonition to the student, emphasizing the importance of not being misled by petty scholars.
  • 生匆遽起立，东方已白，视之正其墓道前也 - Indicates a change in location or perspective, suggesting a shift in the narrative or a symbolic moment.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 能建廟者萬家不一二，能立祠者千家不一二，能設主者百家不一二 - This long parallel sequence must be read as a three-part comparison, emphasizing the rarity of certain acts.
  • 如神依主而不依墓 - This conditional statement must be read as a logical conclusion based on the belief that the spirits are in the ancestral tablet, not the tomb.
  • 百千亿万贫贱之家，其祖妣皆无依之鬼也 - This clause must be read as a statement emphasizing the vast number of poor and humble families and their neglected ancestors.

[637]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 僦居 - Literally 'to rent a residence,' but in this context, it refers to someone living in a rented house or dwelling.
  • 狎 - Means 'to fraternize' or 'to have an intimate relationship,' often with a connotation of familiarity or even romantic involvement.
  • 搴帘 - Literally 'to lift the curtain,' used here as a metaphor for entering a space, often with a sense of grace or elegance.
  • 含笑 - Means 'with a smile,' indicating a friendly or playful demeanor.
  • 旷隔 - Literally 'long separation,' used here to describe the absence of the fox girl.
  • 姑暂避之 - Means 'to temporarily avoid it' or 'to take a temporary retreat.'
  • 化形为小鼠 - Literally 'to transform into a small mouse,' referring to the fox girl's ability to change her form.
  • 大言欺世 - Literally 'to make grandiose claims to deceive the world,' describing someone who pretends to have supernatural powers.
  • 词锋太利 - Literally 'sharp words,' indicating that the fox girl's speech is clever and incisive.
  • 未免不留余地 - Literally 'cannot help but leave no room for maneuver,' suggesting that the fox girl's remarks were too harsh or unyielding.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 道观 - Refers to a Taoist temple or monastery, where Taoist rituals and practices are conducted.
  • 道士 - Refers to a Taoist priest or practitioner of Taoism.
  • 仙 - Refers to an immortal or someone who has achieved spiritual enlightenment, often associated with Taoist beliefs.
  • 静默 - Refers to a state of quietness or silence, often associated with meditation or contemplation in Taoist practice.
  • 颠狂 - Refers to a state of madness or irrational behavior, often used to describe someone who is acting unpredictably or erratically.
  • 伪仙伪佛 - Refers to someone who pretends to be an immortal or a Buddhist monk, often associated with fraud or deception.
  • 文士 - Refers to a scholar or literatus, someone who is well-versed in literature and the arts.
  • 名高 - Refers to someone who is famous or renowned, often for their literary or artistic achievements.
  • 冷峭 - Refers to someone who is cold and aloof, often with a connotation of being unfriendly or distant.
  • 纵酒骂座 - Refers to someone who drinks excessively and behaves rudely, often in a public setting.
  • 张皇 - Refers to someone who is anxious or agitated, often with a connotation of being overly dramatic or theatrical.

Proper Nouns

  • 陈裕斋 - A historical figure, likely a scholar or literatus, who is recounting the story.
  • 钱稼轩先生 - Another historical figure, likely a scholar or literatus, who is present during the conversation and comments on the fox girl's observations.

[638]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 司炊者 - Descriptive title indicating the role of a cook.
  • 其子僧也 - Indicates that her son is a Buddhist monk.
  • 言尝见 - Means 'says' or 'has seen.'
  • 到寺营斋 - Means 'to the temple' and 'to organize a vegetarian meal.'
  • 云其妻亡已十九年 - Means 'says' or 'reports' and 'her wife died nineteen years ago.'
  • 一夕，灯下见形 - Means 'one night' and 'appeared in the form of a shadow under the lamp.'
  • 自到黄泉，无时不忆 - Means 'since arriving in the underworld' and 'never stops thinking of.'
  • 尚冀君百年之后得一相见 - Means 'still hopes' and 'to meet once after a hundred years (i.e., after death).'
  • 不意今配入转轮 - Means 'unexpectantly' and 'assigned to the cycle of rebirth.'
  • 从此茫茫万古，无复会期 - Means 'from now on' and 'vast and endless' and 'no more meeting time.'
  • 故冒冥司之禁，赂监送者，来一取别耳 - Means 'therefore' and 'defying the rules of the underworld' and 'bribing the escort' and 'to come and say goodbye.'
  • 其夫骇痛，方欲致词，忽旋风入室，卷之去，尚隐隐闻泣声 - Means 'shocked and grieved' and 'was about to speak' and 'suddenly a whirlwind entered the room' and 'swept her away' and 'still faintly heard her sobbing.'
  • 故为饭僧礼忏，资来世福也 - Means 'therefore' and 'to feed monks and perform penance' and 'to accumulate merit for the next life.'
  • 此夫此妇，可谓两个不相负矣 - Means 'can be said' and 'two who do not betray each other.'
  • 长恨歌曰：但令心如金钿坚，天上人间会相见 - Quoting a famous line from Bai Juyi's poem, emphasizing the enduring nature of their love.
  • 安知不以此一念，又种来世因耶 - Means 'how can one know' and 'not with this thought' and 'also plant the cause for the next life.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 粤东 - Refers to the eastern part of Guangdong province, a region in southern China.
  • 宦家 - A family of officials or a wealthy household, often associated with high social status.
  • 斋 - A vegetarian meal, often associated with religious or spiritual practices.
  • 黄泉 - The underworld or the realm of the dead.
  • 转轮 - The cycle of rebirth, a concept in Buddhism.
  • 冥司 - The underworld bureaucracy, responsible for the afterlife.
  • 饭僧礼忏 - Feeding monks and performing penance, common Buddhist practices to accumulate merit.
  • 长恨歌 - A famous poem by Bai Juyi, which tells the tragic story of Emperor Xuanzong and Yang Guifei.

Proper Nouns

  • 曹媪 - Cao, the cook.
  • 长恨歌 - A famous poem by Bai Juyi.

[639]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 桂苑丛谈 - A collection of anecdotes or notes.
  • 方竹杖 - A cane made of square bamboo.
  • 大宛国 - An ancient kingdom, now part of modern-day Kazakhstan.
  • 坚实而正方 - Describes the bamboo as solid and square.
  • 节眼须牙，四面对出 - Describes the nodes and teeth of the bamboo, with four sides emerging.
  • 古今注 - A historical or encyclopedic text.
  • 乌孙 - An ancient kingdom, now part of modern-day Kazakhstan.
  • 青田核 - A type of fruit or nut.
  • 六升瓠 - A gourd the size of six sheng (a unit of volume).
  • 空之以盛水，俄而成酒 - Describes the process of turning water into wine.
  • 杜阳杂编 - A historical or encyclopedic text.
  • 芸晖堂 - A hall named 'Yunhui,' possibly referring to a place of study or meditation.
  • 芸香草 - A type of grass with a fragrant aroma.
  • 于阗国 - An ancient kingdom, now part of modern-day Xinjiang.
  • 洁白如玉，入土不朽烂 - Describes the grass as white and pure, not decaying when buried in the ground.
  • 舂之为屑，以涂其壁 - Describes the process of grinding the grass into powder and using it to coat the walls.
  • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions, now part of Central Asia.
  • 玛努根 - A type of grass or plant.
  • 苍术 - A type of herb.
  • 番僧 - Refers to Tibetan or Central Asian monks.
  • 焚以供佛 - Describes the act of burning the plant to offer to the Buddha.
  • 小说附会之词 - Refers to fictional or speculative accounts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 大宛国 - An ancient kingdom known for its horses and other exotic goods.
  • 乌孙 - An ancient kingdom known for its horse breeding and nomadic lifestyle.
  • 于阗国 - An ancient kingdom known for its silk production and cultural significance.
  • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions, which were important in trade and cultural exchange along the Silk Road.
  • 玛努根 - A plant mentioned in Tibetan or Central Asian texts, possibly with medicinal or religious significance.

Proper Nouns

  • 李卫公 - A historical figure, possibly Li Wei.
  • 甘露寺僧 - A monk at Ganlu Temple.
  • 大宛国 - An ancient kingdom, now part of modern-day Kazakhstan.
  • 闽粤 - Refers to the regions of Fujian and Guangdong.
  • 哈萨克 - Refers to the modern-day country of Kazakhstan.
  • 乌孙 - An ancient kingdom, now part of modern-day Kazakhstan.
  • 伊犁地 - Refers to the region of Ili.
  • 额鲁特 - Refers to the Oirat Mongols.
  • 元载 - A historical figure, possibly a high-ranking official.
  • 芸晖堂 - A hall named 'Yunhui.'
  • 于阗国 - An ancient kingdom, now part of modern-day Xinjiang.
  • 和阗地 - Refers to the region of Hetian.
  • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions.
  • 玛努根 - A type of grass or plant.
  • 苍术 - A type of herb.
  • 番僧 - Refers to Tibetan or Central Asian monks.

[641]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 李樵风言 - Standard formula for introducing a narrative or anecdote, meaning 'Li Qiaofeng said.'
  • 泊神祠下 - Means 'moored under the shrine,' where '神祠' refers to a shrine dedicated to a deity.
  • 神诃曰 - Means 'the god rebuked,' indicating a divine rebuke or scolding.
  • 汝曹野鬼 - A dismissive term for 'you lot of wild spirits,' used by the god to address the ghosts.
  • 罪当笞 - Means 'should be punished with a cane,' referring to corporal punishment.
  • 刺刺不止 - A vivid phrase meaning 'chattering incessantly,' often used to describe someone talking too much.
  • 稍稍引去 - Means 'gradually withdrew,' indicating a gradual departure.
  • 非敢有所触犯也 - Means 'we dare not offend in any way,' expressing a plea for forgiveness.
  • 神默然少顷 - Means 'the god remained silent for a moment,' indicating a pause after the rebuke.
  • 论文雅事 - Means 'a refined scholarly activity,' referring to discussing literature or poetry.
  • 择地择人 - Means 'choose the right place and the right people,' suggesting that certain activities should be conducted in appropriate settings.
  • 先生休矣 - A polite way of saying 'please stop,' used by the god to end the discussion.
  • 磷火如萤 - Means 'phosphorescent lights like fireflies,' describing ghostly lights.
  • 吃吃笑不已 - Means 'chuckling continuously,' indicating a light, continuous laughter.
  • 四散而去 - Means 'dispersed and went away,' describing the ghosts leaving.
  • 刘睞 - A personal name, likely referring to a specific individual.
  • 沧州人 - Means 'a person from Cangzhou,' indicating the person's place of origin.
  • 其母以康熙壬申生 - Means 'his mother was born in the year Ren Shen of the Kangxi era,' providing a specific birth year.
  • 至乾隆壬子，年一百一岁 - Means 'by the year Ren Zi of the Qianlong era, she was 111 years old,' indicating her age.
  • 尚强健善饭 - Means 'still strong and healthy, able to eat well,' describing her physical condition.
  • 屡逢恩诏 - Means 'often encountered imperial edicts of grace,' referring to imperial decrees granting favors.
  • 里胥欲为报官支粟帛 - Means 'the village headman wanted to report to the authorities to receive grain and silk,' indicating an offer of assistance.
  • 辄固辞弗愿 - Means 'always firmly declined and did not wish to,' showing her refusal.
  • 去岁，欲为请旌建坊 - Means 'last year, someone wanted to petition for a tablet and a monument,' indicating another offer.
  • 固辞弗愿 - Again, means 'firmly declined and did not wish to,' showing her consistent refusal.
  • 贫家嫠妇 - Means 'a poor widow,' describing her social status.
  • 赋命蹇薄 - Means 'fate was hard and thin,' indicating a difficult life.
  • 颠连困苦 - Means 'suffering from repeated hardships,' describing her struggles.
  • 为神道所怜 - Means 'was favored by the deities,' suggesting divine protection.
  • 得此寿耳 - Means 'only to have lived this long,' explaining why she lived so long.
  • 一邀过分之福 - Means 'if one seeks excessive blessings,' indicating a warning against overreach.
  • 则死期至矣 - Means 'then the time of death will come,' suggesting a negative consequence.
  • 此媪所见殊高 - Means 'this old woman's insight is unusually high,' praising her wisdom.
  • 计其生平 - Means 'considering her life,' referring to her past.
  • 必无胶胶扰扰分外之营求 - Means 'she certainly had no excessive or unnecessary pursuits,' indicating a simple and contented life.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 戰死 - Literally 'died in battle,' indicating a heroic death in combat.

  • 據宋史 - Refers to the *History of Song*, a historical record of the Song dynasty.
  • 奉使 - Means 'to be sent on a mission' or 'to be dispatched as an envoy,' referring to official diplomatic duties.
  • 距 - Means 'to be distant from' or 'to be separated by,' indicating a temporal or spatial gap.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 楊令公 - The title '令公' is a respectful term for a high-ranking official or general, often used posthumously. Here, it refers to General Yang Ye, known for his loyalty and bravery.
  • 宋史 - The *History of Song* is a comprehensive historical record compiled during the Yuan dynasty, covering the history of the Song dynasty.
  • 托克托 - The name '托克托' is a transliteration of the Mongolian name 'Toghtoa,' who was a prominent official in the Yuan dynasty. The note in the text explains that the name was initially transcribed as '脱脱' but was corrected to '托克托' for accuracy.
  • 元季 - Refers to the late Yuan dynasty, a period of political and social turmoil.
  • 三史國語解 - Refers to the *Three Histories National Language Explanation*, a work that provides explanations of historical terms and names in the context of the Three Histories (the *History of Song*, *History of Liao*, and *History of Jin*).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 一夕 - Means 'one night' or 'one evening,' setting the temporal context.
  • 忽问 - Means 'suddenly asked,' indicating a sudden and unexpected question.
  • 人未离形之鬼 - Spirits that have not yet departed from their physical form or ghosts that are still attached to their bodies.
  • 鬼已离形之人耳 - People who have already departed from their physical form or those who are already ghosts.
  • 虽未见之，然觉无可畏 - Although I have not seen them, I feel there is nothing to fear.
  • 其人恧然 - The person felt ashamed or the person blushed.
  • 身即是鬼 - I am a ghost.
  • 以生为士族 - Because I was born into a noble family.
  • 不能逐焰口，争钱米 - Unable to follow the flames (i.e., unable to perform the ritual of feeding the hungry ghosts) and compete for money and grain.
  • 叨为气类 - Gratefully being part of the same kind or gratefully being a part of the same species.
  • 求君一饭可乎 - May I ask for a meal from you?

  • 殊有端委 - Very organized or very well-structured.
  • 偶论太极无极之旨 - Occasionally discussing the principles of Taiji and Wuji.
  • 其人怫然 - The person became angry or the person was indignant.
  • 于传有之 - There is a saying or there is a tradition.
  • 天道远，人事迩 - The ways of heaven are distant, while human affairs are near.
  • 六经所论，皆人事 - The Six Classics discuss all human affairs.
  • 即易阐阴阳，亦以天道明人事也 - Even though the *Yi Jing* explains yin and yang, it also uses the ways of heaven to clarify human affairs.
  • 舍人事而言天道，已为虚杳 - To talk about the ways of heaven instead of human affairs is already too abstract and vague.
  • 又推及先天之先，空言聚讼，安用此为 - Furthermore, discussing what comes before the primordial, empty words lead to disputes; what use is there in this?
  • 谓君留心古义 - You pay attention to ancient meanings.
  • 故就君求食 - That's why I come to you for food.
  • 君所见乃如此乎 - Is this how you see things?
  • 拂衣竟起 - He brushed off his clothes and stood up.
  • 倏已影灭 - Immediately disappeared.
  • 再于相遇处候之 - Waited again at the place where they met.
  • 不复睹矣 - But did not see him again.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 张 - Likely refers to Zhang, a character in the story.
  • 太极无极 - Refers to the philosophical concepts of Taiji (Supreme Ultimate) and Wuji (Infinite), which are central in Chinese metaphysics.
  • 六经 - Refers to the Six Classics: the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals), and *Lunyu* (Analects of Confucius).
  • 士族 - Refers to a noble or scholarly family.
  • 逐焰口 - Refers to the ritual of feeding the hungry ghosts, a Buddhist practice.
  • 气类 - Refers to the same kind or species, often used in a metaphorical sense.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '一夕，忽问君畏鬼乎？' is elliptical and requires the implied subject to be made explicit in English.
  • The sentence '张曰：人未离形之鬼，鬼已离形之人耳，虽未见之，然觉无可畏。' is a direct quote and must be rendered as such.
  • The sentence '其人恧然曰：身即是鬼，以生为士族，不能逐焰口，争钱米，叨为气类，求君一饭可乎？' is another direct quote and must be rendered as such.
  • The sentence '张契分既深，亦无疑惧，即为具食，且邀使数来，考论图籍，殊有端委。' describes a series of actions and must be read as a coherent sequence.
  • The long sentence '偶论太极无极之旨，其人怫然曰：于传有之，天道远，人事迩，六经所论，皆人事，即易阐阴阳，亦以天道明人事也，舍人事而言天道，已为虚杳：又推及先天之先，空言聚讼，安用此为？谓君留心古义，故就君求食，君所见乃如此乎？拂衣竟起，倏已影灭，再于相遇处候之，不复睹矣。' contains multiple clauses and must be read as a coherent narrative.

  • 六经 - The Six Classics.
  • 士族 - A noble or scholarly family.
  • 逐焰口 - A ritual practice.
  • 气类 - A metaphorical term.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 始知之 - Means 'she finally knew' or 'she came to understand,' indicating a moment of realization.
  • 时方坐笔捧楼上，凝立良久 - Describes the woman sitting at her desk in the upstairs room, standing silently for a long time, conveying a sense of deep thought or emotional struggle.
  • 忽对众备言始末，长号数声，奋身投下死 - Dramatic sequence where the woman suddenly recounts her entire story to the crowd, cries out loudly, and then leaps to her death, capturing the intensity of her emotional and physical reaction.
  • 睩柱纲常 - Phrase referring to upholding moral and social norms, often associated with Confucian values.

  • 忍耻偷生 - Means 'to endure shame and live on,' suggesting a compromise of dignity for survival.
  • 苟延一息 - Means 'to cling to life at any cost,' emphasizing the desperation of survival.
  • 冀乐昌破镜，再得重圆 - Metaphor from the story of '破镜重圆' (the broken mirror reunited), where the woman hopes for a reunion with her husband, symbolizing a desire for restoration after separation.
  • 至望绝势穷，然后一死以明志 - Means 'until all hope is lost and circumstances are dire, she finally chooses to die to prove her resolve.'
  • 私爱缠绵 - Refers to the woman's deep, lingering affection for her husband, which complicates her decision to die.
  • 可待不待为辜夫之望 - Means 'to delay or avoid what should have been done is a betrayal of her husband's expectations.'
  • 哀其遇，悲其志，惜其用情之误则可矣 - Complex expression of sympathy for the woman's fate, sorrow for her intentions, and regret for her emotional mistakes.
  • 必执春秋大义，责不读书之儿女，岂与人为善之道哉 - Final statement criticizing those who rigidly apply Confucian principles (春秋大义) to judge the woman, arguing that such judgment is not compassionate or constructive.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • Rou Ting, a scholar in the Hanlin Academy, once visited a friend in Handan. When he arrived, his host was not home, so he temporarily stayed at the City God Temple. Just then, a seller of melons rested his load and lay down in front of the deity's seat. An old man selling thread was staying inside the temple. The old man warned the seller of melons, 'You should not do that; the god is divine.' The seller of melons replied, 'How can the god be in this dilapidated house?' The old man said, 'I often go out at night to cool off and hear voices in the hall. Tiptoeing and listening stealthily, I heard a fox pleading with the god. The general meaning is that a fox from a neighboring house seduced a young man. Just before the young man died, the fox still wanted to take his essence. His family was very angry and set up hunters with guns and arrows to attack it. The fox was frightened, revealed its true form, and ran away. The crowd shouted and followed behind. The fox did not go to its own den but went to a den about a mile away. The people set up nets outside the den, smoked it with fire, and all in the den were killed. But this fox took advantage of the opportunity and escaped. Therefore, it accused them of shifting blame.'

  • 铳矢 - Firearms and arrows.

  • 怂恿柳伺隙杀狐 - Means 'encourages Liu to wait for an opportunity to kill the fox,' indicating the wife's push for action.
  • 负心为歉 - Means 'feels guilty for betraying someone,' describing Liu's reluctance to harm the fox.
  • 彼能媚某家女，不能媚汝女耶 - Means 'If it can charm someone else's daughter, why can't it charm your daughter?' This rhetorical question challenges Liu's loyalty to the fox.
  • 昨以五金为汝女制冬衣 - Means 'Yesterday, it gave five gold pieces to make your daughter a winter coat,' indicating the fox's generosity.
  • 其意恐有在 - Means 'there must be some intention behind it,' suggesting that the fox's actions are not without purpose.
  • 此患不可不除也 - Means 'This problem cannot be left unresolved,' emphasizing the need to take action against the fox.
  • 阴市砒霜 - Means 'secretly buys arsenic,' describing Liu's preparation to poison the fox.
  • 沽酒以待 - Means 'buys wine to wait,' indicating Liu's plan to lure the fox.
  • 先叙相契之深 - Means 'first recounts the depth of their friendship,' describing the fox's recollection of their relationship.
  • 次陈相周之久 - Means 'then mentions the long period of support,' highlighting the fox's contributions to Liu's well-being.
  • 次乃一一发其阴谋 - Means 'then reveals each of its hidden plans,' indicating the fox's disclosure of Liu's intentions.
  • 吾非不能为尔祸 - Means 'I am not incapable of causing you harm,' expressing the fox's capability to retaliate.
  • 然周旋已久，宁忍便作寇仇 - Means 'However, we have been acquainted for a long time, and I cannot easily become your enemy,' showing the fox's reluctance to sever ties.
  • 又以布一匹，棉一束自檐掷下 - Means 'then throws down a piece of cloth and a bundle of cotton from the eaves,' indicating the fox's gesture of goodwill.
  • 昨尔幼儿号寒苦，许为作被，不可失信于孺子也 - Means 'Yesterday, your child cried from cold and hunger, and I promised to make a blanket for them. I cannot break my promise to a child,' explaining the fox's motivation.
  • 众意不平，咸诮让柳 - Means 'The others feel unjustly treated and scold Liu,' describing the reactions of the neighbors.
  • 交不择人，亦吾之过 - Means 'Choosing friends without discrimination is also my fault,' acknowledging the fox's role in the situation.
  • 世情如是，亦何足深尤 - Means 'The world is like this, and there is no need for deep resentment,' expressing the fox's resignation to the nature of human relationships.
  • 太息而去 - Means 'sighs and leaves,' describing the fox's departure.
  • 柳自是不齿于乡党 - Means 'Liu is no longer respected by his fellow villagers,' indicating the social consequences of his actions.
  • 亦无肯资济升斗者 - Means 'No one is willing to help him even with a small amount,' highlighting the isolation Liu faces.
  • 挈家夜遁 - Means 'leaves his family at night,' describing Liu's sudden departure.
  • 竟莫知所终 - Means 'and no one knows his final destination,' indicating the mystery surrounding Liu's fate.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐女 - Refers to a female fox spirit, a common figure in Chinese folklore known for its supernatural abilities and often portrayed as both benevolent and malevolent.
  • 巨室 - Refers to a wealthy family, often associated with high social status and influence.
  • 符录 - Refers to talismans and spells used in traditional Chinese exorcism and magic.
  • 百金 - A significant sum of money in ancient China, often used as a reward for heroic or extraordinary deeds.
  • 砒霜 - A highly toxic substance used in ancient times for poisoning, reflecting the dangerous methods used in the story.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 世传推命始于李虚中 - It is said that the practice of fortune-telling began with Li Xuzhong.
  • 其法用年月日而不用时 - His method uses the year, month, and day but not the hour.
  • 盖据昌黎所作虚中墓志也 - This is based on the epitaph written by Changli (Han Yu) for Li Xuzhong.
  • 其书宋史艺文志著录 - His book is recorded in the *Yiwenzhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) of the *Songshi* (History of Song).
  • 今已久佚 - It has been lost for a long time.
  • 惟永乐大典载虚中命书三卷 - Only the *Yongle Dadian* (Great Canon of the Yongle Era) contains three volumes of Li Xuzhong's *Ming Shu* (Book of Destiny).
  • 尚为完帙 - It is still a complete work.
  • 所说实兼论八字 - What it says actually includes discussions of the eight characters (Ba Zi).
  • 非不用时 - It is not that the hour is not used.
  • 或疑为宋人所伪托 - Some suspect it was fabricated by Song dynasty people.
  • 莫能明也 - It cannot be proven otherwise.
  • 考虚中墓志 - Examining the epitaph of Li Xuzhong.
  • 称其最深于五行 - It states that he was most proficient in the Five Elements.
  • 书以人始生之年月日，所直日辰，支干相生，胜衰死生，互相斟酌 - He recorded the year, month, and day of a person's birth, the day and hour they were born, the relationships between the stems and branches, and the cycles of growth and decline, birth and death, all of which he carefully considered.
  • 推人寿夭贵贱，利不利云云 - He deduced a person's lifespan, wealth, status, and fortune or misfortune, etc.
  • 天有十二辰 - There are twelve hours in a day.
  • 故一日分为十二时 - Therefore, a day is divided into twelve hours.
  • 日至某辰即某时也 - When the sun reaches a certain hour, it is that hour.
  • 故时亦谓之日辰 - Therefore, the hour is also called a day and hour.
  • 国语：星与日辰之位，皆在北维是也 - Refers to a passage in the *Guoyu* (Discourses of the States) that discusses the positions of stars and day-hours in the northern sky.
  • 诗：跂彼织女，终日七襄 - Refers to a verse in the *Shijing* (Book of Songs) that describes the weaving maiden and her daily tasks.
  • 孔颖达疏，从旦暮七辰一移，因谓之七襄 - Kong Yingda's commentary explains that the weaving maiden moves through seven hours from morning to evening, hence the term 'seven shifts'.
  • 楚辞：吉日兮辰良，王逸注，日谓甲乙辰，谓寅卯以辰，与日分言，尤为明白 - Refers to a passage in the *Chuci* (Songs of Chu) with a note by Wang Yi that clarifies the distinction between days and hours.
  • 据此以推 - Based on this, we can infer.
  • 似乎所直日辰四字，当连上年月日为句 - It seems that the four characters '所直日辰' should be connected to the preceding '年月日' as a single phrase.
  • 后人误属下文为句，故有不用时之说耳 - Later people incorrectly connected it to the following text, thus leading to the idea that the hour was not used.

  • 但不识天下一灶神欤，一城一乡一灶神欤，抑一家一灶神欤 - A series of rhetorical questions asking whether there is one Kitchen God for the entire world, one per city or village, or one per family. The use of '抑' (yì) here introduces an alternative.

  • 又不识天下人家如恒河沙数，天下灶神亦当如恒河沙数 - Raises the question of how many Kitchen Gods there are given the vast number of households, using the metaphor of '恒河沙数' (hénghéshāshù) to emphasize the multitude.

  • 或曰：人家立一祀必有一鬼凭之，祀在则神在，祀废则神废 - A possible explanation that each household deity is tied to a specific ritual and disappears when the ritual ends.
  • 不必一一帝所命也 - Suggests that these deities are not individually appointed by the Emperor.
  • 是或然矣 - Concludes that this explanation might be correct.

  • The phrase '此真黎邱之伎矣，青雷曰' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'This is truly the trick of the fox, Zhu Qinglei said.'
  • The phrase '以我所見，好事者當為媒合' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'According to what I saw, those who like to meddle should arrange a match.'
  • The phrase '然聞兩家父母皆恚甚，各延巫驅狐' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'However, I heard that both sets of parents were very angry, and they each invited a witch to drive away the fox spirits.'
  • The phrase '時方束裝北上，不知究竟如何也' is somewhat elliptical. It can be read as 'At the time, I was packing to go north, and I don't know how it turned out in the end.'

  • 蓋侵擾無已 - Formal expression meaning 'continuing harassment or disturbance,' used to introduce the argument that follows.
  • 勢不得不铤而走險 - Means 'the situation forces one to take desperate measures,' indicating a critical turning point.
  • 背城借一 - Classical idiom meaning 'to fight a decisive battle,' often used in military or political contexts.
  • 群狐之力 - Metaphorical expression referring to the combined strength of a group, often used to imply a ragtag or unconventional force.
  • 原不難於殺一人 - Means 'originally it was not difficult to kill one person,' suggesting an initial ease in achieving a goal.
  • 然殺一人易，殺一人而激眾人之怒 - Concessive clause meaning 'but killing one person is easy, but killing one person and thereby provoking the anger of many is...' indicating a shift in complexity.
  • 不焚巢犁穴不止也 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'they will not stop until they destroy the nests and plow the holes,' implying total destruction.
  • 僅使知畏而縱之 - Means 'to merely make them fear and then let them go,' suggesting a policy of leniency.
  • 姑取和焉 - Means 'to temporarily seek peace,' indicating a short-term solution.
  • 後患息矣 - Means 'future troubles will cease,' suggesting that leniency will prevent further conflict.
  • 有力者不尽其力 - Means 'those with power do not exhaust their strength,' suggesting a strategic reserve.
  • 乃可以养其威 - Means 'thereby maintaining their authority,' indicating that restraint preserves power.
  • 屈人者使人易从 - Means 'those who subdue others make it easy for them to submit,' suggesting that dominance facilitates compliance.
  • 乃可以就服 - Means 'thereby achieving submission,' indicating the ultimate goal of dominance.
  • 召陵之役 - Refers to the Battle of Zhaoling, a historical event where the state of Qi intervened in a conflict between the states of Cai and Chu.
  • 不责以僭王，而责以苞茅 - Means 'not holding them accountable for usurping the title of king, but holding them accountable for failing to provide tribute,' indicating a strategic choice in diplomatic demands.
  • 讲学家说春秋者 - Refers to scholars who interpret the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, a classic text of Chinese historiography.

  • 淮西符离之事 - Refers to the Battle of Fuli in Huai West, a historical event where the state of Han suffered a significant defeat.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 隆万以后 - Refers to the period after the reigns of Emperors Longqing (1567-1572) and Wanli (1572-1620) in the Ming dynasty.
  • 流为伪体 - Means 'degenerated into false forms,' criticizing the decline in literary standards.
  • 反唇焉 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to turn against,' indicating criticism or opposition.
  • 挺然自为宗派 - Means 'to stand out and form one's own school,' highlighting individuality and innovation in literature.
  • 根柢 - Refers to the foundation or basis of something.
  • 互诋 - Means 'to mutually criticize,' describing the rivalry and disputes among different literary schools.
  • 董江都、司马文园文格不同 - Refers to the different writing styles of Dong Jiguo (董仲舒) and Sima Xiangru (司馬相如), both prominent figures in Han dynasty literature.
  • 李杜王孟诗格不同 - Refers to the different poetic styles of Li Bai (李白), Du Fu (杜甫), Wang Wei (王維), and Meng Haoran (孟浩然), all renowned Tang dynasty poets.
  • 所得者深焉耳 - Means 'they have gained deeply,' emphasizing the profound understanding and skill of these writers.
  • 后之学者，论甘则忌辛，是丹则非素 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'later scholars, who praise what is sweet and criticize what is bitter, who accept red but reject white,' criticizing the narrow-mindedness and bias of later scholars.
  • 惜不尽闻其说也 - Means 'regretting not hearing the rest of his explanation.'
  • 平心之论 - Means 'an impartial opinion,' indicating a balanced and fair assessment.
  • 托诸鬼魅语 - Means 'to entrust it to the words of ghosts and spirits,' a literary device used to avoid direct criticism.
  • 无庸歇后 - Means 'no need for a punchline,' referring to the technique of not needing to explain the full meaning after a metaphor.
  • 永年百无一长 - Means 'I have no virtue at all,' a humble self-assessment.
  • 不能作妄语 - Means 'cannot speak falsely,' indicating honesty and integrity.
  • 亦不敢固争 - Means 'I也不敢坚持争论,' indicating a reluctance to argue against the other person's opinion.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 隆万以后 - Refers to the late Ming dynasty, a period of significant cultural and political change.

  • 司马文园 - Sima Xiangru (司馬相如), a famous poet and writer during the Han dynasty.
  • 李杜王孟 - Li Bai (李白), Du Fu (杜甫), Wang Wei (王維), and Meng Haoran (孟浩然), all prominent Tang dynasty poets.
  • 长沙一派 - Refers to a literary school or group associated with Changsha, possibly a reference to a specific literary movement or group of writers.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence introduces the decline in literary standards and the subsequent criticism by the Changsha school.
  • This complex sentence discusses the formation and eventual decline of literary schools, highlighting the initial strength and subsequent weaknesses.
  • These sentences compare the different writing styles of various literary figures and note that they did not criticize each other despite their differences.
  • This phrase emphasizes the deep understanding and skill of the earlier writers.
  • This metaphorical expression criticizes the narrow-mindedness and bias of later scholars.
  • This phrase expresses regret at not hearing the full explanation.
  • This sentence provides a commentary on the use of metaphor and the avoidance of direct criticism.
  • This sentence captures a dialogue where one person expresses self-deprecation and another responds with a mix of skepticism and respect.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蓋狐女假形攝其精 - Compound phrase describing the fox spirit's ability to disguise herself and drain energy.
  • 一夕所耗已多也 - Literal meaning 'the amount drained in one night is already much,' emphasizing significant energy loss.
  • 前納妾者聞之 - '前' means 'former' or 'previous,' referring to someone who previously took a concubine. '聞之' means 'heard it' or 'learned about it.'
  • 亦抵以書曰 - '亦' means 'also' or 'too.' '抵以書' means 'replied in a letter,' where '抵' means 'to reply' or 'to respond,' and '書' means 'letter.'
  • 夫婦居室，不能謂之不正也 - '夫婦居室' refers to the act of husband and wife being intimate. '不能謂之不正' means 'cannot be called improper,' suggesting that the act itself is not inherently wrong.
  • 狐魅假形，亦非意料之所及也 - '狐魅假形' means 'the fox spirit assuming a form.' '亦非意料之所及' means 'is also not something one could have anticipated.'
  • 然一夕而大損真元，非恣情縱欲不至是 - '然' means 'however' or 'but.' '一夕而大損真元' means 'drained a great deal of true essence in one night.' '非恣情縱欲不至是' means 'it would not have happened without unrestrained indulgence.'
  • 無乃燕昵之私，尚有不節以禮者乎 - '無乃' is a rhetorical particle meaning 'is it not' or 'is it that.' '燕昵之私' refers to intimate relations. '尚有不節以禮者乎' means 'is there still something lacking in moderation and propriety?'
  • 且妖不勝德，古之訓也 - '且' means 'furthermore' or 'moreover.' '妖不勝德' means 'evil cannot overcome virtue.' '古之訓也' means 'an ancient teaching.'
  • 周張程朱不聞曾有遇魅事 - '周張程朱' refers to the Neo-Confucian scholars Zhou Dunyi, Zhang Zai, Cheng Hao, Cheng Yi, and Zhu Xi. '不聞曾有遇魅事' means 'were never known to have encountered a fox spirit.'
  • 而此魅公然犯函丈 - '而' means 'but' or 'however.' '公然' means 'openly' or 'blatantly.' '犯函丈' means 'to violate the respect due to a teacher,' where '函丈' is a respectful term for a teacher.
  • 無乃先生之德，尚有所不足乎 - '無乃' is a rhetorical particle meaning 'is it not' or 'is it that.' '先生之德' means 'the virtue of the teacher.' '尚有所不足乎' means 'is there still something lacking?'
  • 先生賢者也，責備賢者，春秋法也 - '先生賢者也' means 'the teacher is a virtuous person.' '責備賢者' means 'to hold a virtuous person to high standards.' '春秋法也' means 'it is the way of the *Spring and Autumn Annals*,' referring to the moral and ethical standards of Confucianism.
  • 朋友規過之義，不敢不以告 - '朋友規過之義' means 'the duty of friends to correct each other's faults.' '不敢不以告' means 'I dare not fail to inform you.'
  • 此生得書，但力辩实无此事，里人造言而已 - '此生得书' means 'the person received the letter.' '但力辩实无此事' means 'but strongly argued that there was no such thing.' '里人造言而已' means 'it was just fabricated by villagers.'
  • 宋清远先生闻之曰：此所谓以子之矛，陷子之盾 - '宋清远先生闻之曰' means 'Mr. Song Qingyuan heard about it and said.' '此所谓以子之矛，陷子之盾' is a metaphorical expression meaning 'this is like using your own spear to pierce your own shield,' referring to self-defeating arguments.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 狐女 - Refers to a fox spirit, a common figure in Chinese folklore known for seducing humans.
  • 狐魅 - Another term for a fox spirit, often associated with supernatural abilities.
  • 真元 - Refers to the vital essence or life force in Chinese philosophy.
  • 恣情縱欲 - Refers to unrestrained indulgence in desires, often associated with moral corruption.
  • 燕昵之私 - Refers to intimate relations, often used in a moral context.
  • 節以禮 - Means 'moderation and propriety,' a key Confucian value.
  • 周張程朱 - Refers to the Neo-Confucian scholars Zhou Dunyi, Zhang Zai, Cheng Hao, Cheng Yi, and Zhu Xi, who are highly respected for their moral and intellectual contributions.
  • 函丈 - A respectful term for a teacher, emphasizing the teacher-student relationship.
  • 春秋法 - Refers to the moral and ethical standards of Confucianism, as outlined in the *Spring and Autumn Annals*.
  • 里人 - Refers to villagers or common people, often used to contrast with scholars or officials.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This section discusses the need to read complex sentences as single units to understand the context, such as '蓋狐女假形攝其精，一夕所耗已多也' and '前納妾者聞之，亦抵以書曰'.
  • It also mentions the importance of breaking down complex sentences into clear, readable English while preserving the logical flow and structure, as seen in the clauses '夫婦居室，不能謂之不正也，狐魅假形，亦非意料之所及也' and '然一夕而大損真元，非恣情縱欲不至是'.
  • The section emphasizes the need to explain the cause of the energy loss and link it to moral behavior, as in '無乃燕昵之私，尚有不節以禮者乎' and '且妖不勝德，古之訓也'.
  • It also highlights the contrast between the behavior of the fox spirit and the expected behavior of virtuous scholars, as in '周張程朱不聞曾有遇魅事，而此魅公然犯函丈'.
  • The section concludes with a metaphorical comment on the self-defeating nature of the argument, as in '宋清远先生闻之曰：此所谓以子之矛，陷子之盾'.

Proper Nouns

  • 宋清远先生 - Refers to Mr. Song Qingyuan, a historical figure known for his moral and intellectual contributions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吴必大录曰 - A citation from Wu Bidai, a recorder or compiler of stories, introducing a secondary source.
  • 因论薛士龙家见鬼 - Refers to a discussion about seeing ghosts in the home of Xue Shilong, a topic of interest in the context of belief in supernatural phenomena.
  • 世之信鬼神者，皆谓实有，在天地间 - A statement about those who believe in ghosts and spirits, asserting their existence in the world.
  • 其不信者，断然以为无鬼 - Refers to skeptics who firmly deny the existence of ghosts.
  • 然却又有真个见者 - Acknowledges that there are people who claim to have genuinely seen ghosts.
  • 郑景望遂以薛氏所见为实 - States that Zheng Jingwang took Xue Shilong's sightings as true, showing a specific case of belief.
  • 不知此特虹霓之类耳 - A skeptical conclusion that what was seen was likely just a rainbow or similar natural phenomenon.
  • 问虹霓只是气，还有形质 - A question about whether rainbows are just vapor or have substance.
  • 曰：既能啜水，亦必有肠肚 - A response suggesting that if rainbows can absorb water, they must have some form of internal structure.
  • 只才散便无 - Means 'only when they disperse do they disappear,' reinforcing the idea that rainbows are ephemeral.
  • 如雷部神亦此类 - Compares rainbows to thunder gods, both being transient and possibly illusory phenomena.
  • 世之见鬼者甚多 - States that many people claim to have seen ghosts.
  • 不审有无如何 - A question about whether these sightings are real or not.
  • 曰：世间人见者极多，如何谓无，但非正理耳 - A response stating that since many people claim to have seen ghosts, they cannot be dismissed, but the sightings are not based on sound reasoning.

  • 虹霓 - Rainbows, often used in classical Chinese literature to symbolize natural phenomena that can be mistaken for supernatural events.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 佛自西域而来 - Literal expression meaning 'Buddha came from the Western Regions,' referring to the introduction of Buddhism to China from Central Asia.
  • 其空虚清净之义 - Refers to the Buddhist concept of emptiness and purity, which can be interpreted as the idea of detachment from material desires and the pursuit of inner peace.
  • 驰鹜者息营求 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'those who rush about cease their pursuits,' suggesting that those who are overly active or driven will find peace by ceasing their relentless striving.
  • 忧愁者得排遣 - Means 'those who are worried can find relief,' indicating that Buddhism can provide solace to those who are troubled.
  • 因果报应之说 - Refers to the Buddhist doctrine of cause and effect, which teaches that actions have consequences.
  • 警戒下愚 - Means 'to warn the ignorant and foolish,' suggesting that Buddhism serves as a moral guide for those who lack wisdom.
  • 回心向善 - Means 'to turn one's heart toward goodness,' indicating a moral transformation.
  • 于世不为无补 - Means 'is not without benefit to the world,' suggesting that Buddhism has a positive impact on society.
  • 挟技之食客 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'a guest with skills,' suggesting that Buddhism is like a guest who brings valuable skills or knowledge to a host.
  • 食客不修其本技 - Means 'the guest does not refine his own skills,' suggesting that Buddhism does not focus on its own core teachings.
  • 变更主人之家政 - Means 'to change the master's household affairs,' suggesting that Buddhism tries to alter the fundamental practices of the host.
  • 使主人退而受教 - Means 'to make the master step back and receive instruction,' suggesting that Buddhism attempts to teach the host, which is inappropriate.
  • 佛者之过也 - Means 'it is the fault of Buddhism,' suggesting that Buddhism is at fault for its approach.
  • 各以末流而论 - Means 'considering the various branches,' suggesting that the discussion will focus on the different schools or interpretations of Buddhism.
  • 譬如种田 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'like farming,' suggesting that the discussion will use the analogy of agriculture to explain the relationship between Buddhism and Confucianism.
  • 儒犹耕耘者也 - Means 'Confucianism is like the farmer,' suggesting that Confucianism is the foundational and practical aspect of Chinese culture.
  • 佛家失其初旨 - Means 'Buddhism has lost its original purpose,' suggesting that Buddhism has deviated from its core teachings.
  • 不以善恶为罪福 - Means 'does not consider good and evil as causes of punishment and reward,' suggesting that Buddhism has shifted away from moral judgment.
  • 而以施舍不施舍为罪福 - Means 'but considers giving and not giving as causes of punishment and reward,' suggesting that Buddhism has focused on material charity instead of moral virtue.
  • 惑众蠹财 - Means 'deceiving the masses and wasting resources,' suggesting that Buddhism has led to the misallocation of resources and the spread of false beliefs.
  • 侵越疆畔 - Means 'trespassing boundaries,' suggesting that Buddhism has overstepped its proper role.
  • 攘窃禾稼 - Means 'stealing crops,' suggesting that Buddhism has taken resources that belong to others.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions, which were the areas west of China, including parts of Central Asia and India.
  • 空虚清净 - Refers to the Buddhist concepts of emptiness (śūnyatā) and purity, which are central to Buddhist philosophy.
  • 因果报应 - Refers to the Buddhist doctrine of karma, which teaches that actions have consequences.
  • 下愚 - Refers to the ignorant and foolish, a term often used in Confucian texts to describe those who lack moral and intellectual development.
  • 回心向善 - Refers to the moral transformation that is encouraged in both Buddhism and Confucianism, but the text suggests that Buddhism has not been effective in this regard.
  • 末流 - Refers to the various branches or schools of Buddhism, which have developed over time and may have diverged from the original teachings.
  • 种田 - Refers to farming, a common metaphor in Chinese literature to describe the cultivation of virtue and the foundation of society.

Proper Nouns

  • 佛 - Refers to Buddhism.
  • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions.
  • 中国 - Refers to China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 本集皆不載 - Not included in the collection, indicating that the poems were omitted from the official anthology.
  • 诋诃前辈 - To criticize or rebuke one's predecessors, a term that carries a negative connotation, suggesting disapproval or harsh criticism.
  • 微涉讦直 - Slightly involving direct accusation or confrontation, indicating a tone of confrontation or directness that might be considered inappropriate.
  • 编集时自删之欤 - Was it deleted during the compilation? This phrase suggests that the omission was deliberate, possibly due to the content's controversial nature.
  • 赠庆大司马丹年 - Presented to Grand Marshal Danian, where 'Grand Marshal' is a high-ranking military title, and 'Danian' is likely a personal name or title.
  • 刘石庵参知颇疑其伪 - Liu Shianan, the Censor-in-Chief, suspected it was fake, indicating skepticism about the authenticity of the text.
  • 古人多有集外诗 - Ancient people often had poems outside their collections, suggesting that it was not uncommon for poets to have works that were not included in their published anthologies.
  • 当是孙公传庭所亲制 - It is likely made by Sun Chuanjing himself, indicating that the authorship is attributed to a specific person.
  • 二砚大小相近 - The two stones are similar in size, describing the physical characteristics of the stones.
  • 姚安公以皆前代名臣，合为一匣 - Grampa Yao, considering they were both famous ministers from previous dynasties, put them together in one box, showing the respect and value placed on these items.
  • 汝佶夭逝 - Rujie died young, indicating a premature death.
  • 二砚为婢媪所窃卖 - The two stones were stolen and sold by maidservants, suggesting a loss due to theft.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 二诗本集皆不载 - Indicates that the poems were not included in the official collection, possibly due to their controversial nature.
  • 诋诃前辈 - Criticizing or rebuking one's predecessors was a sensitive topic in classical Chinese culture, often seen as disrespectful.
  • 微涉讦直 - Direct confrontation or accusation was generally frowned upon in classical Chinese society, especially when directed at respected figures.
  • 编集时自删之欤 - The process of compiling literary works was often meticulous and selective, and omissions could be deliberate.
  • 赠庆大司马丹年 - Grand Marshal was a high-ranking military position, and the act of presenting a gift to such a figure was significant.
  • 刘石庵参知颇疑其伪 - The role of Censor-in-Chief was to oversee and critique government officials, and suspicion of forgery was a serious charge.
  • 古人多有集外诗 - Many ancient poets had unpublished works, which sometimes revealed more personal or controversial aspects of their lives.
  • 当是孙公传庭所亲制 - Sun Chuanjing was a historical figure known for his contributions to literature and governance.
  • 二砚大小相近 - The similarity in size of the stones suggests a deliberate pairing or matching.
  • 姚安公以皆前代名臣，合为一匣 - The act of combining items from different eras in a single container shows a respect for historical continuity and the value of these items.
  • 汝佶夭逝 - Premature death was a common theme in classical Chinese literature, often evoking sympathy and reflection.
  • 二砚为婢媪所窃卖 - Theft by servants was a common problem in classical Chinese households, often leading to the loss of valuable items.

  • 姨丈惕园牛公言 - Formal introduction of a narrative, meaning 'Aunt's husband, Mr. Ti Yuan Niu, said.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '一奴子业针工，其父母鬻身时，未鬻此子，故独别居于外' introduces the background of the servant and his living situation.
  • The sentence '其妇年二十余，为狐所媚，岁余病瘵死' describes the woman's encounter with the fox and her subsequent death.
  • The sentence '初不肯自言，病甚，乃言狐初来时为女形，自言新来邻舍也' explains the woman's initial reluctance to speak about the encounter and her eventual disclosure.
  • The sentence '留与语，渐涉谑，继而渐相逼，遽前拥抱，遂昏昏如魇' describes the progression of the fox's interactions with the woman.
  • The sentence '每夜辄来，必换一形，忽男忽女，忽老忽少，忽丑忽好，忽僧忽道，忽鬼忽神，忽今衣冠忽古衣冠' lists the various forms the fox takes.
  • The sentence '岁余无一重复者' emphasizes the uniqueness of each transformation.
  • The sentence '至则四肢缓纵，口噤不能言，惟心目中了了而已' describes the physical and mental state of the woman during the encounters.
  • The sentence '狐亦不交一言，不知为一狐所化，抑众狐更番而来也' raises the question of whether one fox or multiple foxes were involved.
  • The sentence '尤怪者，小姑偶入其室，突遇狐出，一跃即逝' describes a particularly strange encounter involving the woman's younger sister-in-law.
  • The sentence '小姑所见是方巾道袍人，白须瞏瞏，妇所见则黯黑垢腻，一卖煤人耳' describes the different appearances of the fox to the two women.
  • The sentence '同时异状，更不可思议耳' emphasizes the extreme strangeness of the event.
